preamble: The voiced labial palatalization shift from [b] to the voiced palatal stop [ɟ] is a rare but phonetically documented process, particularly in Southern Bantu languages. Similar to its voiceless counterpart [p] > [c], this shift involves the fronting of a labial articulation toward the palatal region under the influence of high front vowels or glides.

examples.note: Bantu passive and diminutive formations often trigger palatalization of labials, where /b/ shifts toward [ɟ] (or further to [dʒ]).
In some varieties, the labial-glide cluster /bj/ develops into a palatal stop.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Schenker, A. M. (1995). The Dawn of Slavic.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
